_note: P2 full-library curation (#475). Complements seed_attack_exemplar_mappings.json. Rules: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-05-attack-curation-p2-design.md §Curation methodology. Deliberate gap: entry 'generative-ai-prompt-injection' stays unmapped pending the MITRE ATLAS domain (#482) — no Enterprise nearest-fit.

mappings.rationale: Entry description names 'mis-setting a SharePoint/cloud-share permission, leaving a database or mailbox publicly accessible' as the exposure mechanism. This entry has no adversary or motive term (threat_actor_type=insider_accidental) -- the mapping documents only that the resulting exposed object is the same cloud-storage access surface T1530 describes an adversary exploiting, not that an intrusion chain occurred; other error modes in the entry (misdelivered email, wrong attachment) have no corresponding Enterprise technique and are intentionally left unmapped.

Entry description states cybercriminals redirect payments 'to attacker-controlled accounts' via BEC targeting 'grain-elevator disbursements' -- the load-bearing account-compromise/impersonation step needed to convincingly redirect an established payment relationship; tags include 'bec'.

Entry's attack_vector field is 'phishing'; description frames the scheme as cybercriminals 'using business email compromise techniques' timed to 'grain planting and harvest cycles' to open the fraud chain.

Entry description states the objective is to 'redirect large commodity payments, input-supply invoices, or grain-elevator disbursements to attacker-controlled accounts' -- the defining financial-theft impact of this archetype.

Entry describes hacktivists who 'tamper with navigation receivers, firmware, or connectivity modules' on autonomous farm equipment -- physically introducing a GPS-spoofing or connectivity-interception device at the receiver/connectivity module is the hardware-manipulation vector this entry frames, consistent with tags 'gps_spoofing' and 'autonomous_equipment'.

Entry states the tampering is intended 'to disrupt planting/harvesting operations' -- denying the equipment operator's ability to properly direct autonomous, GPS-guided machinery once navigation, firmware, or connectivity are compromised is the operator-control-denial impact this technique covers on the tractor's process-control loop.

CircleCI's own incident report states 'an unauthorized third party leveraged malware deployed to a CircleCI engineer's laptop in order to steal a valid, 2FA-backed SSO session' via 'session cookie theft, enabling them to impersonate the targeted employee' -- the infostealer-malware session-theft behavior this entry names among 'TravisCI 2022 and CircleCI 2023' as a side-channel to secrets exposure.
citations: CircleCI, 'CircleCI incident report for January 4, 2023 security incident' (2023-01-13), https://circleci.com/blog/jan-4-2023-incident-report/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: The same CircleCI report states the stolen session let the actor 'impersonate the targeted employee in a remote location and then escalate access to a subset of our production systems' -- reuse of the stolen session as alternate authentication material to move into production, bypassing CircleCI's normal access controls.
citations: CircleCI, 'CircleCI incident report for January 4, 2023 security incident' (2023-01-13), https://circleci.com/blog/jan-4-2023-incident-report/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: CircleCI's report states that, via the compromised employee's production-token-generation privileges, the actor was able to 'access and exfiltrate data from a subset of databases and stores, including customer environment variables, tokens, and keys' -- harvesting insecurely-exposed customer secrets from CircleCI's internal stores, the CI-provider-breach secrets exposure this entry describes as distinct from secrets committed directly to source control.
citations: CircleCI, 'CircleCI incident report for January 4, 2023 security incident' (2023-01-13), https://circleci.com/blog/jan-4-2023-incident-report/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: FBI IC3 PSA I-091124-PSA documents that BEC 'is frequently carried out when a subject compromises legitimate business or personal email accounts through social engineering or computer intrusion' -- the account-takeover behavior this entry describes as 'sophisticated account takeover of legitimate supplier email accounts.'
citations: FBI IC3 PSA I-091124-PSA, 'Business Email Compromise: The $55 Billion Scam' (2024-09-11), https://www.ic3.gov/PSA/2024/PSA240911 (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states attacks 'range from simple display-name spoofing... to sophisticated account takeover of legitimate supplier email accounts' -- display-name-spoofed sender deception is the phishing-style lure that opens the BEC chain; attack_vector=email_social_engineering.

The same IC3 PSA documents the compromise is conducted 'to conduct unauthorized transfers of funds' -- the direct financial-theft impact this entry describes as redirecting 'wire transfers or ACH payments to attacker-controlled accounts.'
citations: FBI IC3 PSA I-091124-PSA, 'Business Email Compromise: The $55 Billion Scam' (2024-09-11), https://www.ic3.gov/PSA/2024/PSA240911 (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry describes cybercriminals who 'physically connect external attack hardware (black-box devices or USB-connected controllers) to the ATM's dispensing mechanism.' Recorded Future News's reporting on the FBI's 2026 ATM-jackpotting alert confirms the underlying physical-tampering pattern -- criminals open the ATM cabinet and have 'the malware interact[] directly with the ATM hardware, bypassing any communications or security of the original ATM software' -- via a hard-drive swap rather than this entry's black-box/USB device, a different specific implement for the same physical hardware-manipulation objective.
citations: Recorded Future News (The Record), 'FBI: More than 700 ATM jackpotting incidents with losses over $20 million occurred in 2025' (2026), https://therecord.media/fbi-atm-jackpotting-2025-report (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry describes attackers 'forcing the machine to dispense cash on command without a legitimate transaction.' Recorded Future News's reporting on the FBI's 2026 jackpotting alert confirms that malware exploiting the ATM's XFS layer lets attackers 'instruct the ATM to dispense cash on demand' and that 'the malware does not require connection to an actual bank customer account to dispense cash' -- the same unauthorized cash-extraction-for-gain behavior.
citations: Recorded Future News (The Record), 'FBI: More than 700 ATM jackpotting incidents with losses over $20 million occurred in 2025' (2026), https://therecord.media/fbi-atm-jackpotting-2025-report (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Per Dragos, TRISIS added a new command to the TriStation protocol to reprogram the safety controller's execution -- the same controller-reprogramming behavior this entry attributes to a nation-state actor defeating a chemical plant's SIS.
citations: Dragos, 'TRISIS Takeaways: Defensive Techniques and Trench-Building for the Blue Team', https://www.dragos.com/blog/industry-news/trisis-takeaways-defensive-techniques-and-trench-building-for-the-blue-team/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Dragos's TRISIS analysis documents the malware manipulating outputs and executing new logic on the Triconex safety controller; this entry generalizes the same TRITON-pattern SIS manipulation to a chemical/petrochemical process driving overpressure or runaway conditions.
citations: Dragos, 'TRISIS Takeaways: Defensive Techniques and Trench-Building for the Blue Team', https://www.dragos.com/blog/industry-news/trisis-takeaways-defensive-techniques-and-trench-building-for-the-blue-team/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Dragos documents TRISIS altering safety logic even with the keyswitch in RUN/REMOTE mode, circumventing the safeguard against unauthorized changes -- the SIS-defeat objective this entry describes as opening a 'catastrophic-consequence tail' once the safety barrier is gone.
citations: Dragos, 'TRISIS Takeaways: Defensive Techniques and Trench-Building for the Blue Team', https://www.dragos.com/blog/industry-news/trisis-takeaways-defensive-techniques-and-trench-building-for-the-blue-team/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states the attacker 'establishes persistent access to the cloud environment' after obtaining or bypassing cloud provider credentials -- ongoing abuse of the compromised IAM/Azure AD/GCP account is the access mechanism connecting initial credential theft to follow-on activity; attack_vector=stolen_credentials_or_mfa_bypass.

Entry description states attackers 'begin exfiltration or cryptomining' once persistent cloud access is established, and example_incidents names 'Multiple AWS cryptomining campaigns via exposed IAM keys 2021-2024' -- cryptomining is the resource-hijacking impact this entry names as an alternative monetization path to data theft.

The same Krebs report states the intruder 'used those creds to make API calls to the S3 service and dump all the data' -- direct collection of data from cloud storage using the stolen IAM credentials, the S3-enumeration-and-exfiltration conclusion of the Capital One 2019 pattern this entry names in its description.
citations: Krebs on Security (Brian Krebs), 'What We Can Learn from the Capital One Hack' (2019-08-02), https://krebsonsecurity.com/2019/08/what-we-can-learn-from-the-capital-one-hack/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Krebs on Security documents the Capital One intruder exploiting a misconfigured WAF's SSRF flaw so the WAF would 'send a request to the local metadata service, which returned the temporary IAM credentials the WAF was using' -- harvesting insecurely-exposed cloud credentials from the instance metadata service, the credential-theft step this entry names as the 'SSRF→IAM-role-credential pattern'.
citations: Krebs on Security (Brian Krebs), 'What We Can Learn from the Capital One Hack' (2019-08-02), https://krebsonsecurity.com/2019/08/what-we-can-learn-from-the-capital-one-hack/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states 'modern attackers immediately... disable GuardDuty/CloudTrail' upon gaining cloud access -- tampering with the cloud environment's detection/logging tooling to impair visibility of follow-on data theft or cryptomining.

The same press release documents a two-step sequence -- Levandowski downloaded the 14,000 SVN files onto his Google-issued laptop on 2015-12-11, then 'transferred those SVN files from his Google-issued laptop to his personal laptop' three days later -- the local collection/staging step preceding removal from employer control; entry names 'potentially misappropriated files' as the loss vehicle. Mapped narrowly: the entry's canonical_fair_gap itself notes the primary attack vector is 'a job offer (not a phishing email)', so no ATT&CK initial-access technique is force-fitted here -- only the data-handling steps the primary actually documents.
citations: US DOJ, Northern District of California, 'Former Uber Executive Sentenced To 18 Months In Jail For Trade Secret Theft From Google' (2020-08-04), https://www.justice.gov/usao-ndca/pr/former-uber-executive-sentenced-18-months-jail-trade-secret-theft-google (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: The DOJ press release on Levandowski's sentencing documents he 'downloaded approximately 14,000 files from an internal, password-protected Google server known as "SVN"' -- the internal engineering-data repository this entry's Waymo v. Uber example_incidents references, matching Data from Information Repositories.
citations: US DOJ, Northern District of California, 'Former Uber Executive Sentenced To 18 Months In Jail For Trade Secret Theft From Google' (2020-08-04), https://www.justice.gov/usao-ndca/pr/former-uber-executive-sentenced-18-months-jail-trade-secret-theft-google (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states 'successful matches grant account takeover' once stuffing succeeds -- the attacker's operation of the now-valid customer credentials is the load-bearing pivot from automated guessing to actual account control.

The New York Attorney General's press release on the Dunkin' settlement describes the attacks as 'repeated, automated attempts to gain access to accounts using usernames and passwords stolen through security breaches of other unrelated websites or online services' -- the automated credential-stuffing behavior this entry names as its defining tooling (Sentry MBA, Snipr, Openbullet) testing breached credentials against the consumer portal.
citations: New York Office of the Attorney General, 'Attorney General James Gets Dunkin' to Fill Holes in Security, Reimburse Hacked Customers' (2020-09-15), https://ag.ny.gov/press-release/2020/attorney-general-james-gets-dunkin-fill-holes-security-reimburse-hacked-customers (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states attackers 'drain loyalty points, redirect payment instruments, make fraudulent purchases, or sell valid account credentials' post-takeover -- direct monetary/technical theft executed from the compromised consumer accounts.

AA21-200A documents APT40 actors 'establish initial access via user and administrator credentials' -- the credential-based access this entry's long-dwell intrusion model relies on once the external network foothold is gained.
citations: CISA/FBI Joint Cybersecurity Advisory AA21-200A, 'Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures of Indicted APT40 Actors Associated with China's MSS Hainan State Security Department' (2021-07-20, updated 2021-07-22), https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa21-200a (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: CISA/FBI AA21-200A documents APT40's tactics 'include spearphishing, exploitation of public-facing applications' as initial-access methods; this entry's attack_vector=external_network_exploitation and 'long-dwell intrusions' framing extends that documented APT40 behavior to a crop-science target, which AA21-200A itself lists 'research institutes' and 'biomedical' among APT40's targeted industries (not crop-science specifically -- extended here per the entry's archetype).
citations: CISA/FBI Joint Cybersecurity Advisory AA21-200A, 'Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures of Indicted APT40 Actors Associated with China's MSS Hainan State Security Department' (2021-07-20, updated 2021-07-22), https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa21-200a (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: AA21-200A documents APT40 actors 'locate high value assets in order to exfiltrate data' after lateral movement -- the collection step this entry describes as exfiltrating 'proprietary seed genetics, pesticide and herbicide formulation data, and precision-agriculture algorithms' from R&D repositories.
citations: CISA/FBI Joint Cybersecurity Advisory AA21-200A, 'Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures of Indicted APT40 Actors Associated with China's MSS Hainan State Security Department' (2021-07-20, updated 2021-07-22), https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa21-200a (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry's attack_vector field is 'external_network_exploitation.' This entry's own description and example_incidents (British Airways/Magecart web-skimming, Marriott/Starwood long-dwell compromise, T-Mobile) do not share a single stated intrusion mechanism -- the entry is a consequence/secondary-loss archetype (the regulatory notification cascade AFTER a breach), not an attack-chain description, so this row maps only the generic external-network-exploitation vector the entry's own field names, without inventing a specific chain from any one named incident.

Entry description states adversaries gain unauthorized access 'via tailgating, cloned access badges, or social engineering of facility staff' -- the cloned-badge/social-engineered-credential path is the perimeter-access-control bypass this entry frames; tags include 'tailgating'.
Entry description states adversaries who gain physical access 'install network taps or hardware implants' -- the direct hardware-introduction behavior this technique covers; tags include 'implant'.

Entry states 'Mirai and MerisBot botnets have demonstrated multi-terabit attack capability.' Cloudflare's retrospective analysis documents Mirai's attack module implementing 'UDP flooding, and all TCP flooding options' and reaching '623 Gbps' (Krebs on Security) and peaking at '1TBs' (OVH) -- the multi-terabit network-layer volumetric flood capability this entry names.
citations: Cloudflare Blog, 'Inside the infamous Mirai IoT Botnet: A Retrospective Analysis' (2017-12-14), https://blog.cloudflare.com/inside-mirai-the-infamous-iot-botnet-a-retrospective-analysis/, 'Attack module' section (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry's example_incidents names 'Meris botnet DDoS against Yandex 2021 (21.8M RPS peak).' Qrator Labs' technical analysis documents the Meris botnet's HTTP-pipelining technique reaching '21.8 M rps' against Yandex -- an application-layer request flood exhausting the target web service rather than raw network bandwidth, matching this entry's Meris-specific example.
citations: Qrator Labs, 'Meris botnet, climbing to the record' (2021-09-09), https://blog.qrator.net/en/meris-botnet-climbing-to-the-record_142/, narrative following the 'Discovery' section (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry describes hacktivists 'breaching server rooms' among the three listed tampering actions ('cutting network cables, disabling emergency-communication systems, or breaching server rooms'); unauthorized physical entry to a server room is the access precondition this technique's hardware-introduction behavior presupposes. The entry does not state what, if anything, was introduced once inside, and the cable-cutting/comms-disabling actions have no distinct ATT&CK enterprise equivalent, so this is the entry's only mapped row.

Entry's attack_vector field names the chain as 'spearphishing_followed_by_lateral_movement' -- valid-account reuse is the standard mechanism bridging an initial researcher-workstation foothold to the file servers and research repositories the entry describes as the ultimate target.

The FBI/CISA joint PSA on PRC targeting of COVID-19 research documents PRC-affiliated actors 'attempting to identify and illicitly obtain valuable intellectual property (IP) and public health data... from networks and personnel affiliated with COVID-19-related research' -- the pre-publication research-data theft this entry describes, extended from COVID-specific research data to the entry's broader university research-repository framing.
citations: FBI/CISA Joint Public Service Announcement, 'People's Republic of China (PRC) Targeting of COVID-19 Research Organizations' (2020-05-13), https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/publications/Joint_FBI-CISA_PSA_PRC_Targeting_of_COVID-19_Research_Organizations_S508C.pdf (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: CISA AA21-200B documents Chinese state-sponsored actors sent 'fraudulent spearphishing emails that were buttressed by fictitious online profiles and contained links to doppelganger domain names' -- the spearphishing-of-researchers behavior this entry names as the attack chain's first step; attack_vector=spearphishing_followed_by_lateral_movement.
citations: CISA/FBI/NSA Joint Cybersecurity Advisory AA21-200B, 'Chinese State-Sponsored Cyber Operations: Observed TTPs' (2021-07-19), https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa21-200b (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states the insider 'abuses access to student information systems (SIS/ERP)' -- the entry's threat_actor_type is insider_malicious with an already-provisioned SIS/ERP account as the load-bearing access vector, not an externally obtained credential.

Entry description states the insider exfiltrates 'student personal records, academic performance data, financial-aid information, or student-health service records' from the SIS/ERP -- data mined from a shared institutional information repository rather than a single local system.

CISA/FBI/MS-ISAC advisory AA25-163A documents ransomware actors exploiting CVE-2024-57727, a path-traversal vulnerability allowing 'unauthenticated remote attackers to download arbitrary files from the SimpleHelp host,' to compromise a utility billing software provider's internet-facing RMM -- a real instance of the external network exploitation of billing-adjacent infrastructure this entry's attack_vector names; the advisory documents ransomware/double-extortion rather than this entry's covert meter-fraud objective, which this entry extends the initial-access step to support.
citations: CISA/FBI/MS-ISAC Cybersecurity Advisory AA25-163A, 'Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider' (2025-06-12), https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-163a (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states the attacker manipulates 'meter readings, billing records, or consumption data... enabling fraudulent under-billing, theft of service, or energy diversion at scale' -- unauthorized alteration of the revenue-accounting data itself, the entry's defining fraud mechanism. (Investigated the PREPA/Krebs 2012 case named for AMI fraud precedent generally; that campaign used physical optical-port access to meters, not the network exploitation this entry describes, so it is not used as grounding here.)

Entry description frames the attack as targeting 'the energy-trading or wholesale-settlement platform (ETRM, ISO/RTO scheduling interface, or bilateral PPA settlement system)'; attack_vector=external_network_exploitation names the initial-access path into this externally-reachable business platform.

Entry description names 'a data-tampering or availability attack' where 'settlement windows expire without valid data' -- an availability-denial dimension of the same platform attack distinct from the corruption dimension, consistent with tags including 'data_tampering' alongside the entry's explicit 'availability attack' framing.

Entry description states the attack 'corrupts or halts the settlement process' where 'invoices are corrupted' -- unauthorized alteration of the settlement/billing data underlying the offtaker's contracted revenue, this entry's defining data-tampering mechanism.

Entry's example_incidents names Stuxnet 2010, which 'manipulated centrifuge process parameters while feeding controllers prior recorded values' -- an in-line interception of the field-to-controller signal path (Adversary-in-the-Middle), distinct from the HMI-display falsification the entry explicitly excludes ('distinct from manipulation-of-view').

Description states 'the control loop is steered toward an out-of-spec state' by the falsified measurements -- a process-parameter-level impact consistent with Modify Parameter, driven here by corrupted inputs rather than a direct parameter write.

Entry describes 'spoofing or biasing sensor inputs... or actuator feedback so controllers and operators act on physically false measurements,' i.e. falsified reporting/command messages injected below the HMI -- the field-level message-spoofing behavior this technique covers (v19 successor to the retired Spoof Reporting Message / Unauthorized Command Message pair).

Entry's canonical_fair_gap states the 'primary loss profile [is] dominated by authorized transfer fraud (the agent is deceived into authorizing a legitimate-looking transfer)' -- the institution's own funds are stolen via the manipulated transfer, matching tags 'authorized_transfer' and 'vishing'.

Entry description states cybercriminals 'call financial institution customer service centers posing as customers, law enforcement, or internal staff to manipulate agents into authorizing fraudulent wire transfers, resetting account authentication, or disclosing account information' -- influencing call-center agents to take unauthorized action is the defining vishing/pretexting behavior; attack_vector=social_engineering.

Entry description states the insider has 'privileged access to core banking systems, wire-transfer platforms, or trading systems' -- the insider's own legitimate privileged account, exploited to bypass dual control, is the load-bearing access vector; attack_vector=insider_privileged_access.

Entry description states typical methods include 'backdating transactions, altering beneficiary fields, inserting fictitious trades, or bypassing dual-control authorization' -- direct insertion/alteration of transaction records to divert funds, matching Data Manipulation's insert/manipulate-data-to-influence-outcomes definition. No technique-evidencing primary was found for this archetype: the entry's own source_citations is empty and the named example (Societe Generale/Kerviel 2008) is documented in French regulatory and court records that could not be fetched and behavior-confirmed at authoring time, so this row is left expert-estimate rather than force-citing a loss-magnitude-only source.

Entry describes ransomware 'encrypting ERP systems, warehouse management systems, and OT-adjacent temperature-monitoring and process-control networks' -- the direct encryption impact this entry's regulatory-tail gap analysis is built on.

attack_vector=phishing and tags include 'ransomware'/'ot_adjacent'; entry names cybercriminal ransomware deployment against food manufacturing facilities beginning from a phishing-based foothold before reaching ERP/WMS/monitoring networks.

Entry's canonical_fair_gap states compromise of 'temperature-monitoring or food-safety record systems may trigger a mandatory recall even if the ransomware payload never touches the food itself,' and the description names 'extended production shutdown' as a loss driver -- a production/regulatory revenue-loss impact on the food-manufacturing process matching ATT&CK for ICS's Loss of Productivity and Revenue, distinct from the IT-side encryption event itself.

Entry description states the insider 'alters food-safety records -- passing contaminated product, suppressing test failures, or manipulating lot-traceability data' in ERP/LIMS/QA systems -- directly matching Data Manipulation's insert/delete/manipulate-to-influence-outcomes behavior. Per the entry's own example_incidents note, Peanut Corporation/Blue Bell/Jensen Farms are physical-contamination cases with insider-knowledge elements, NOT cyber-tampering incidents, so they are not usable as technique-evidencing citations here (source_citations is empty on this entry) -- expert-estimate only, grounded in the entry's own description.

NETSCOUT documents that 'In October 2022, a DDoS cyberattack took down dozens of state government websites including those of Connecticut, Colorado, Mississippi, and Kentucky,' attributed to Killnet -- the volumetric-DDoS-on-citizen-portal behavior this entry names in its example_incidents ('Killnet 2022-2023... DDoS campaigns against multiple US state government portals').
citations: NETSCOUT (Gary Sockrider), 'DDoS Attacks Against Governments Are on the Rise' (2023-08-08), https://www.netscout.com/blog/ddos-attacks-against-governments-are-rise (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states hacktivists mount 'volumetric OR application-layer DDoS attacks against government citizen-service portals' -- the application-layer half of that stated dichotomy, distinct from the volumetric/network-flood row above.

Entry's example_incidents names both 'Harold Martin (NSA contractor, 2016...)' and 'Reality Winner (NSA contractor, 2017...)'. DOJ's press release on Martin states he 'was a contractor with the federal government and had a top secret national security clearance'; DOJ's press release on Winner states she was a federal contractor holding a Top Secret clearance who 'admitted intentionally identifying and printing the classified intelligence reporting despite not having a "need to know"' -- in both cases the insider's own authorized clearance/account, not a compromised credential, is the load-bearing access vector for the leak.
citations: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of Public Affairs, 'Maryland Man Charged With Removal of Classified Materials and Theft of Government Property' (2016-08-29), https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/maryland-man-charged-removal-classified-materials-and-theft-government-property (accessed 2026-07-05), paragraph describing Martin's contractor status and top secret clearance

U.S. Department of Justice, Office of Public Affairs, 'Federal Government Contractor in Georgia Charged With Removing and Mailing Classified Materials to a News Outlet' (2017-06-05), https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/federal-government-contractor-georgia-charged-removing-and-mailing-classified-materials-news (accessed 2026-07-05), paragraph describing Winner's Top Secret clearance and lack of need-to-know

mappings.rationale: Entry description names the exfiltrated record types as 'personnel files, law-enforcement databases, citizen benefit records, or national-security information' -- data resident in shared government information systems/repositories rather than a single workstation's files, consistent with mining information repositories prior to disclosure.

Entry description states the insider 'intentionally corrupts, alters, or deletes official records' in voter-roll, property, criminal-history, court, or benefits systems -- directly matching Data Manipulation's 'insert, delete, or manipulate data... to influence external outcomes or decision making.' No further technique is honestly supportable: attack_vector=insider_access_abuse describes an insider using ALREADY-HELD administrative access (not obtained/escalated via an attack step), so Valid Accounts is not invoked here.

ESET reports the breaker-control payload was 'designed to gain direct control of switches and circuit breakers at an electricity distribution substation' to cut power -- the load-loss availability impact this entry describes ('operator-visible load loss').
citations: ESET WeLiveSecurity (Robert Lipovsky), 'Industroyer: Biggest threat to industrial control systems since Stuxnet' (2017-06-12), https://www.welivesecurity.com/2017/06/12/industroyer-biggest-threat-industrial-control-systems-since-stuxnet/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

citations: ESET WeLiveSecurity (Robert Lipovsky), 'Industroyer: Biggest threat to industrial control systems since Stuxnet' (2017-06-12), https://www.welivesecurity.com/2017/06/12/industroyer-biggest-threat-industrial-control-systems-since-stuxnet/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

citations: CISA, 'ICS Focused Malware (Update A)', ICS-ALERT-14-176-02A, https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/ics-alerts/ics-alert-14-176-02a (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry description states the actor compromises 'a website frequently visited by employees of a target industry (trade association portals, regulatory body websites, industry conference sites)' -- identifying which sites a target industry's workforce visits is reconnaissance of the victim community, matching Gather Victim Org Information.

Krebs on Security's analysis states the Capital One intruder used 'a well-known method called a Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attack, in which a server (in this case, CapOne's WAF) can be tricked into running commands that it should never have been permitted to run' -- exploitation of a weakness in an internet-facing application, matching this entry's SSRF vector; the entry's SQLi/broken-access-control/unauthenticated-API variants are the same class of public-facing-application weakness this technique covers, which this entry extends beyond the SSRF-specific Capital One case.
citations: Krebs on Security (Brian Krebs), 'What We Can Learn from the Capital One Hack' (2019-08-02), https://krebsonsecurity.com/2019/08/what-we-can-learn-from-the-capital-one-hack/ (accessed 2026-07-05)

mappings.rationale: Entry describes the objective as reading 'the backing data store' once broken access control / IDOR or SQL injection is exploited -- collection from the application's own database/repository, the generic non-cloud-storage variant of this entry's data-access step (distinct from the S3-specific Capital One case below).

Krebs documents that the misconfigured WAF role 'was assigned too many permissions, i.e. it was allowed to list all of the files in any buckets of data, and to read the contents of each of those files,' enabling exfiltration of roughly 106 million records from Capital One's S3 buckets -- the cloud-storage-specific collection step this entry names in its Capital One example_incident.
